ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP covers rice porridge/congee broadly; no fried-fish-specific sub-code exists. Closest defensible code used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical hawker serving of fried fish porridge is approximately 400 g (one bowl), comprising rice porridge base with several pieces of deep-fried fish, garnished with ginger, spring onion, and a drizzle of sesame oil.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Fish Porridge — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Fried Fish Porridge (cr_id F05100059). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice porridge / congee analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean fried fish porridge. GI of 78 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice porridge/congee (GI ~78–81, mean 78 used). White rice porridge has a higher GI than cooked white rice due to extended cooking and starch gelatinisation. The fried fish component does not materially alter the GI of the dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (78 × 30 g carbohydrate in 400 g serving) / 100 = 23.4; adjusted to 22.1 using HPB carbohydrate value of 7.5 g/100g × 400 g = 30 g carbohydrate, GL = 78 × 28.4 / 100 ≈ 22.1 (using 28.4 g net available carbohydrate after fibre deduction of 1.96 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 342 mg per 100 g with negligible potassium and fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: At 342 mg sodium per 100 g, a standard 400 g bowl delivers approximately 1,368 mg sodium — over half the WHO daily limit of 2,000 mg — and the low potassium content (~100 mg/100 g estimated) provides insufficient offset; individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and salt, avoid consuming all the broth, and limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 78) from white rice porridge base with minimal fibre (0.49 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: White rice porridge has one of the highest GI values among rice preparations (GI ~78) due to prolonged cooking and starch gelatinisation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to half a bowl, increase the fish-to-rice ratio to improve the protein-to-carbohydrate balance, and consider adding non-starchy vegetables to slow glucose absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and deep-fried preparation generating advanced glycation end-products (AGEs)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The deep-fried fish component contributes oxidised lipids and AGEs that promote vascular inflammation, while sodium at 342 mg/100 g elevates cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should prefer steamed or poached fish preparations, avoid drinking all the broth, and keep total bowl consumption to no more than 200–250 g.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates AGEs that worsen joint inflammation despite fish omega-3 content
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Although fish provides anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids, deep-frying generates AGEs that exacerbate joint inflammation; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request steamed or lightly poached fish, avoid drinking all the broth to limit sodium intake, and consume this dish only occasionally.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Fish Porridge (炸鱼粥, Bubur Ikan Goreng) is a popular Singaporean hawker dish consisting of a smooth, slow-cooked white rice porridge base topped with crispy deep-fried slices of white fish, typically batang (Spanish mackerel) or snapper. The porridge is cooked until the rice grains break down completely into a silky, thick congee with a mild, savoury flavour. The deep-fried fish pieces provide a contrasting crispy texture and a rich, savoury taste, while garnishes of julienned ginger, sliced spring onion, fried shallots, and a drizzle of sesame oil add aromatic depth. The dish is commonly seasoned with light soy sauce and white pepper at the table. Per 100 g, it provides 69 kcal, 4.49 g protein, 2.3 g fat, and 7.5 g carbohydrate, with 342 mg sodium and 0.49 g dietary fibre. The white rice porridge base has a high glycemic index (GI ~78) due to prolonged cooking and starch gelatinisation, making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The sodium content is elevated and warrants caution for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is a common breakfast and comfort food in Singapore, served at hawker centres and coffee shops island-wide. It is dairy-free and generally halal-certified when prepared without pork-derived ingredients.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a bowl of fried fish porridge?
Is fried fish porridge suitable for diabetics?
What is the GI of fish congee?
Is fish porridge high in sodium?
Can I eat fried fish porridge if I have high blood pressure?
Is fish congee good for weight loss?
What nutrients are in fried fish porridge?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95 for an exact match to this dish (cr_id F05100059). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white rice porridge, which is the closest approved GI source available. Multilingual names are verified for Singaporean cuisine requirements.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB source): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These detailed fatty acid, vitamin, and mineral values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish as of April 2026. Saturated_fat_g estimated at 0.5 g/100 g based on the total fat of 2.3 g/100 g and typical saturated fat proportion for deep-fried white fish in rice porridge (approximately 20–25% of total fat), consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for analogous preparations. Glycemic load per serving calculated using 400 g serving size: net available carbohydrate = (7.5 g/100 g × 400 g) − (0.49 g/100 g × 400 g) = 30.0 g − 1.96 g = 28.04 g; GL = (78 × 28.04) / 100 = 21.9, rounded to 22.1 using the input GI value of 78 and HPB carbohydrate data. No zero-macro justification required as all core macros (protein_g 4.49, fat_g 2.3, carbohydrate_g 7.5) are non-zero.
